primerTitle: Modern C++ (C++17 / C++20 / C++23) — quick recap
primer:
## C++17
**Structured bindings** unpack a tuple/pair/struct into named variables: `auto [it, inserted] = m.insert(...);`, `auto [x, y] = point;`, `for (auto& [key, val] : m)` — replacing `std::get<0>` / `.first` / `.second`. **`std::optional<T>`** represents a value that may or may not be present, without sentinels or pointers — `if (result)` to check, `*result` to read — replacing error-prone conventions like returning `-1` or a nullable pointer. **`std::variant`** is a type-safe tagged union holding exactly one of a fixed set of types (`variant<int, std::string>`), queried with `std::holds_alternative`/`std::get` or `std::visit`; safer than a raw `union` because it tracks the active type. **`if constexpr`** is compile-time branching in templates — only the taken branch is instantiated, so the other may be invalid for that type (replacing tag dispatch / SFINAE). **`std::filesystem`** standardised a portable API for paths and files (`path`, directory iteration, `exists`, `create_directory`, `file_size`).

## C++20
**Concepts** are named, checkable constraints on template parameters (`template <std::integral T>`, `requires` clauses); they make requirements explicit and give **clearer errors** than SFINAE/`enable_if` — a failed concept names the unmet requirement instead of a deep instantiation backtrace. **Ranges** redesign algorithms around whole ranges and composable, lazy **views**: `auto r = v | std::views::filter(even) | std::views::transform(square);` — pipelines compose without intermediate containers. The **spaceship operator `<=>`** is a single three-way comparison; `auto operator<=>(const T&) const = default;` lets the compiler generate all six relational operators (`<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`) at once. **Coroutines** are suspendable/resumable functions (`co_await`, `co_yield`, `co_return`) for writing async or lazy/generator code in sequential style. **Modules** replace textual `#include`: `import std;` is imported, not pasted — faster builds, no macro leakage, real encapsulation. **`consteval`** marks an 'immediate function' that *must* run at compile time on every call (stronger than `constexpr`, which only *may*).

## C++23
Incremental polish: **`std::expected<T,E>`** (a value-or-error return type that carries an error value `E` on failure, not just 'empty' like `optional`), **`std::print`/`std::println`** (formatted printing on C++20's `std::format`), multidimensional `operator[]`, **`std::mdspan`**, **deducing `this`**, and **`import std;`** standard-library module support. The theme is ergonomics.

## Exercise mapping
Old patterns → modern replacements: returning `-1` for 'not found' → **`std::optional`** (C++17); hand-writing `operator<`/`operator==` → defaulted **`<=>`** (C++20); `std::get<0>(t)`, `std::get<1>(t)` → **structured bindings** (C++17).
name: C++ — Modern: C++17, C++20 & C++23
description: What the later standards introduced and why they improve on older code: C++17 (structured bindings, optional/variant, if constexpr, filesystem), C++20 (concepts, ranges, coroutines, modules, the spaceship operator) and C++23 highlights.

cards.definition: Syntax to unpack a tuple, pair or struct into named variables: `auto [it, inserted] = m.insert(...);` or `auto [x, y] = point;`. It replaces clumsy `std::get<0>` / `.first` / `.second` access and reads far more clearly, especially with `map` iteration `for (auto& [key, val] : m)`.
cards.summary: `auto [a, b] = pair;` — destructure into named variables.

cards.sub: A) it represents a value that may or may not be present, without sentinels or pointers    B) it makes values immutable    C) it is a faster vector    D) it replaces references
cards.definition: Correct: A) it represents a value that may or may not be present, without sentinels or pointers. `std::optional<int> find(...)` clearly says 'maybe an int', checked with `if (result)` and read via `*result`. It replaces error-prone conventions like returning `-1`, a magic value, or a nullable pointer.

A type-safe tagged union: a value that holds exactly one of a fixed set of types, e.g. `std::variant<int, std::string>`. You query it with `std::holds_alternative`/`std::get` or visit it with `std::visit`. Safer than a raw `union` because it tracks which type is active.
cards.summary: Type-safe union holding one of several types; track the active one.

cards.definition: Compile-time branching inside templates: only the taken branch is instantiated, so the other may contain code invalid for that type. It replaced tag dispatch and SFINAE tricks for choosing behaviour by type, e.g. `if constexpr (std::is_integral_v<T>) ... else ...`.
cards.summary: Compile-time if; only the taken branch is compiled.

cards.definition: A portable API for paths and files: `std::filesystem::path`, directory iteration, `exists`, `create_directory`, `file_size`, etc. Before, file-system work meant platform-specific calls or third-party libraries; now it is in the standard library.
cards.summary: Portable standard API for paths and the file system.

cards.definition: Named, checkable constraints on template parameters: `template <std::integral T>` or `requires` clauses. They make template requirements explicit, give clear error messages when a type doesn't satisfy them, and replace verbose SFINAE/`enable_if` machinery.
cards.summary: Named constraints on template parameters; clearer errors than SFINAE.

cards.definition: A redesign of algorithms around ranges and composable, lazy 'views': `auto r = v | std::views::filter(even) | std::views::transform(square);`. You pass a whole range (not a begin/end pair), and view pipelines compose without intermediate containers. More readable and less error-prone than raw iterator pairs.
cards.summary: Composable, lazy view pipelines over whole ranges (the | syntax).

cards.sub: A) a single three-way comparison the compiler can use to generate all six relational operators    B) a pointer operator    C) a new arithmetic operator    D) a way to launch threads
cards.definition: Correct: A) a single three-way comparison the compiler can use to generate all six relational operators. Declaring `auto operator<=>(const T&) const = default;` gives you `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `==`, `!=` at once, instead of hand-writing each — far less boilerplate and fewer inconsistencies.

Functions that can suspend and resume, using `co_await`, `co_yield` and `co_return`. They let you write asynchronous or lazy/generator code in a sequential style instead of callback chains or hand-rolled state machines — the compiler transforms the function into a resumable state machine.

They replace the textual `#include` model. A module is imported (`import std;`) rather than textually pasted, which avoids re-parsing headers repeatedly, removes macro leakage across boundaries, and can dramatically cut build times. They give real encapsulation of what a component exports.
cards.summary: import instead of #include — faster builds, no macro leakage.

cards.term: Concepts give clearer compile errors than the old SFINAE / enable_if approach.
cards.summary: True — a failed concept names the unmet requirement instead of a wall of template noise.
cards.definition: True. When a type fails a concept, the compiler reports which named requirement was not met, rather than the deep, cryptic instantiation backtraces that SFINAE produced. That readability is a main reason concepts were added.

cards.term: In C++20 you can mark a function {{consteval}} to require it run at compile time on every call — an 'immediate function', stronger than constexpr.
cards.summary: consteval = must be evaluated at compile time (immediate function).
cards.definition: Where `constexpr` *may* run at compile time, `consteval` *must*: every call is evaluated during compilation or it is an error. Useful for compile-time-only factories and checks.

C++23 was an incremental polish: `std::expected<T,E>` (a value-or-error return type), `std::print`/`std::println` (formatted printing built on C++20's `std::format`), multidimensional `operator[]`, `std::mdspan`, deducing `this`, and `import std;` standard-library module support. The theme is ergonomics and rounding out C++20.
cards.summary: std::expected, std::print, mdspan, deducing this, import std.

cards.sub: A) it carries an error value on failure, not just 'empty'    B) it is faster    C) it can hold many values    D) it replaces exceptions entirely
cards.definition: Correct: A) it carries an error value on failure, not just 'empty'. `std::optional` only tells you a value is missing; `std::expected<T,E>` holds either the result `T` or an explanatory error `E`, giving a lightweight, exception-free way to return success-or-reason-for-failure.

cards.term: Exercise: which standard's feature replaces each old pattern? (a) returning -1 to mean 'not found'; (b) hand-writing operator< and operator== separately; (c) `std::get<0>(t)`, `std::get<1>(t)`.
cards.sub: Map each pattern to its modern replacement and standard.
cards.definition: (a) `std::optional<T>` (C++17) makes 'no value' explicit instead of a magic -1. (b) The defaulted spaceship `operator<=>` (C++20) generates all comparisons at once. (c) Structured bindings `auto [a, b] = t;` (C++17) unpack a tuple into named variables. Each modern feature removes a sentinel, boilerplate, or unclear index.
